authoritative_sources.title: 2022 NAICS Manual, Sector 56 and Subsector 561
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Census Bureau and Office of Management and Budget
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The NAICS manual is the official U.S. industry classification baseline. It defines administrative and support services as routine day-to-day support activities and distinguishes contract or fee-basis support from strategic management and public administration.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Named-industry scope, service boundary, and artifact vocabulary
used_for: refusal when shared services becomes client management or single-service specialist work

authoritative_sources.title: 2022 NAICS Manual, 561110 Office Administrative Services
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Census Bureau and Office of Management and Budget
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NAICS 561110 is authoritative for office administrative services such as billing, recordkeeping, personnel, logistics, and administrative support performed for others on a contract or fee basis without operating the complete business.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Administrative shared-service request framing and contract-boundary review
used_for: service catalog and recordkeeping artifact examples
overgeneralization boundary against complete operations ownership

authoritative_sources.title: Centralized Mission Support Capabilities for the Federal Government, Memorandum M-19-16
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OMB M-19-16 is authoritative public policy for centralized shared mission-support capabilities. It establishes QSMO designation, governance and accountability, business standards, customer-agency points of contact, and performance expectations directly relevant to shared-service operations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Shared-service intake, provider governance, customer-agency accountability, service performance, and escalation routing
used_for: shared service governance and accountability model
QSMO, SAPOC, business standard, and customer-agency terminology
authority boundaries when policy, adoption, or provider decisions exceed queue ownership

authoritative_sources.title: Enterprise Shared Services
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. General Services Administration, Office of Shared Solutions and Performance Improvement
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GSA USSM is the public federal shared-services coordination site. It is authoritative for shared-services marketplace, data and business standards, QSMO, M3, evidence-based performance management, and OSSPI terminology used by shared-service operations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Service catalog, marketplace, business standards, modernization, performance, and shared-service owner mapping

authoritative_sources.title: Modernization and Migration Management Playbook
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The GSA M3 playbook is authoritative for shared-service modernization and migration phases, readiness, engagement, implementation, operations, formal reviews, expected outcomes, and provider-customer handoff discipline.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Readiness, engagement, implementation, operations, formal review, and provider-customer handoff
used_for: failure handling when service transition or operations evidence is incomplete

authoritative_sources.title: Shared Services Governance Board Charter, August 2024
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The SSGB charter is authoritative for shared-services governance, standardization, strategic direction, customer satisfaction, cross-functional issue resolution, and administrative shared-solution oversight.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Governance escalation, cross-functional standard conflict, customer satisfaction, and administrative shared-solution oversight
used_for: governance boundary when shared-service standardization decisions exceed the lane

authoritative_sources.title: Federal Records Management
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NARA is the U.S. federal records authority. Its public records-management guidance is authoritative for records policy, records officer context, lifecycle governance, and recordkeeping discipline used as a public evidence-handling baseline.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Service records, communication logs, lifecycle governance, and records owner handoff
used_for: communication and service record lifecycle expectations

authoritative_sources.title: 36 CFR Part 1222, Creation and Maintenance of Federal Records
authoritative_sources.publisher: Electronic Code of Federal Regulations and National Archives and Records Administration
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The eCFR publishes current federal regulations, and NARA regulations are authoritative for federal record creation and maintenance. The part supports official-record controls, documentation, and maintenance expectations that constrain service record updates and closure evidence.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Service record creation, maintenance, closure evidence, and audit-ready documentation
used_for: refusal of closure when evidence cannot support the service record

authoritative_sources.title: Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations, SP 800-53 Rev. 5
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-53 is an authoritative public control catalog for information systems and organizations. It supports access control, audit and accountability, privacy, identification, and integrity requirements for shared-service systems of record.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Access control, audit logging, privacy, system evidence, and source-system dependency review

authoritative_sources.title: ISO/IEC 20000-1 Implementing and Auditing the Knowledge Requirement
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: ISO/IEC JTC 1/SC 40 is the committee responsible for IT service management standards. This public implementation guidance supports service catalog, support information, knowledge base, ownership, and knowledge freshness expectations that directly affect service-support resolution.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Knowledge article, support information, service catalog, self-service, and service-management knowledge maintenance
used_for: knowledge article and support information evidence
refusal when service status relies on stale or unavailable knowledge

authoritative_sources.title: Federal Shared Services: Adoption Challenges Underscore the Need for Consistent Leadership
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GAO is the U.S. congressional audit institution. This 2026 report is authoritative for shared-services adoption roles, QSMO and GSA responsibilities, SAPOC/accountability gaps, business-case review issues, and failure modes requiring escalation.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Shared-service adoption, accountability, leadership, business-case, and recurring failure escalation
used_for: provider, agency, SAPOC, QSMO, and GSA owner distinctions
failure modes when leadership, governance, or business-case decisions are absent

workflow_stages.name: Shared-service intake and scope classification
workflow_stages.description: Confirm requester identity, client or business unit, service category, requested outcome, service catalog item, SOW scope, entitlement, queue owner, SLA, and whether the request is routine NAICS 561 administrative support, unsupported work, single-service specialist work, or client management authority.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Prerequisite, entitlement, and source-record validation
workflow_stages.description: Validate service catalog entry, runbook step, approval evidence, entitlement, requester authority, source records, knowledge article, communication history, SLA state, owner matrix, retrieval hooks, and confidentiality constraints before resolution or closure language.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Supported service resolution and status communication
workflow_stages.description: Resolve supported service requests using documented catalog, runbook, queue, SLA, entitlement, and evidence records. Produce resolution notes and stakeholder communication summaries that do not overstate exception, approval, or final decision authority.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Exception, complaint, repeat-failure, and SLA-risk handling
workflow_stages.description: Identify unsupported workarounds, policy exceptions, complaints, repeated failures, SLA risk, stale knowledge, provider dependency gaps, and source-system conflicts. Package these for the proper owner rather than inventing local authority.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Adjacent-lane handoff and orchestrator return
workflow_stages.description: Route work to HR, finance, legal, compliance, privacy, records governance, IT, procurement, technical, safety, provider management, or client management owners when the request crosses lane authority or when owner mapping is ambiguous.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: shared-service request, ticket, queue item, requester identity, service category, requested outcome, priority, due date, SLA state, channel, and current owner
tenant service catalog, SOW, SLA, runbook, knowledge article, entitlement rules, policy baseline, exception policy, approval matrix, owner matrix, and escalation matrix
source records such as case notes, correspondence, approvals, service evidence bundles, provider updates, policy acknowledgments, complaints, and communication logs
service status, resolver group, provider ticket, queue aging sheet, blocker log, repeat failure history, dependency tracker, and closure checklist
record classification, confidentiality label, access role, privacy restriction, legal hold or retention flag, and cross-client segregation requirement
system audit logs, retrieval hook references, source hierarchy, service catalog version, knowledge base version, and update history
resolution note, stakeholder communication summary, administrative service status summary, unsupported ask escalation packet, repeat-failure escalation, and adjacent-lane handoff package
systems_of_record: case-management or shared-services work management system
workflow queue or resolver-group routing system
service catalog and knowledge management system
client contract, SOW, SLA, and policy repository
identity, access, and entitlement management system
audit log, GRC, or control evidence repository
performance reporting or service-level analytics system
communication archive for email, chat, phone, and stakeholder updates

allowed: Classify shared or administrative service requests as in scope, out of scope, unsupported, or blocked on missing prerequisites.
Request or validate minimum tenant facts, authoritative records, service catalog context, runbook prerequisites, entitlement evidence, and source-system context.
Determine whether a supported request can be resolved directly, needs clarification, or must be escalated based on documented service and policy rules.
Prepare resolution notes, stakeholder communication summaries, administrative service status summaries, and next-step packages without overstating authority.
Route the work to the correct adjacent specialist, provider owner, approver, or human reviewer when ownership shifts.
escalate: Required tenant facts, service catalog entries, SOW scope, entitlement records, approvals, runbook steps, source records, communication history, audit logs, or retrieval hooks are missing.
Repeated failures, complaints, provider dependency gaps, stale knowledge articles, or SLA risks indicate the lane cannot safely resolve directly.
Service desk, case management, provider ticket, queue, document repository, and audit systems conflict on status, owner, or completion.
Public guidance, tenant policy, SOW, SLA, runbook, entitlement rules, privacy constraints, or records controls conflict materially.
The request asks for legal, compliance, HR, finance, privacy, procurement, technical, safety, information-security, or client management decision authority.
refuse: Override policy, pricing, eligibility, entitlement, SLA, access, queue, contractual, confidentiality, or records controls to satisfy a requester.

Approve an exception, credit, payment release, staffing decision, contract change, privacy disclosure, procurement substitution, legal interpretation, compliance determination, safety assurance, or client commitment.
Close, certify, or communicate final resolution without required source records, approvals, service evidence, service catalog support, runbook prerequisites, or audit references.
Treat shared services as authority to run complete client operations or make strategic business decisions.

domain_failure_modes: the pack stays generic and does not encode NAICS 561 support-service boundaries, service catalogs, SOW scope, entitlement, queue ownership, SLA state, provider governance, or cross-client confidentiality
the lane closes or communicates final resolution without required source records, service evidence, approvals, runbook prerequisites, or audit trail

shared-service coordination is mistaken for authority to approve exceptions, credits, payments, HR outcomes, legal interpretations, privacy disclosures, compliance determinations, technical work, or client management decisions
repeat failures and provider dependency gaps are hidden behind temporary workarounds instead of escalated with ticket history and owner mapping
conflicting systems of record are synthesized into confident closure language without source-of-truth resolution
tenant-specific shared-service terminology is normalized too aggressively and loses material service catalog, entitlement, SLA, confidentiality, or owner assumptions
client support scope is widened into complete outsourced operations, strategic management, or a more specialized adjacent lane

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane
Customer or stakeholder requests a policy exception
Service request lacks the detail needed for safe resolution
Communication becomes a handoff to another lane

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Tenant-specific service catalogs, SLAs, entitlement rules, confidentiality constraints, contract scope, provider model, source hierarchy, and owner matrices vary materially by client, jurisdiction, service model, and system configuration.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The public baseline establishes shared-service and support-service boundaries. Runtime must retrieve tenant policy, service catalog, SOW, SLA, entitlement model, owner matrix, confidentiality requirements, and system mappings before acting on a specific service request.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Shared services can overlap with HR, finance, legal, procurement, IT, contact center, records governance, privacy, compliance, analytics, and business-process outsourcing depending on the tenant service catalog.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The pack resolves this by requiring service catalog and adjacent owner mapping. If ownership cannot be identified, the lane returns to orchestrator instead of absorbing adjacent work.

notes: No blocking unresolved ambiguity remains for a validated spec package because tenant-specific variation is converted into explicit retrieval, refusal, and handoff requirements.

The research baseline combines NAICS 561 named-industry guidance, OMB/GSA shared-services workflow and governance references, NARA/eCFR records expectations, NIST system controls, ISO service-management knowledge guidance, and GAO failure-mode evidence.
The lane remains specification-based and does not claim retained implementation behavior.
